question:
Sea otters live in most coastal areas of the Pacific Ocean. Photograph A shows a sea otter feeding on a sea urchin. Photograph B shows kelp. The statements give information about sea otters and some other organisms in their food web: Kelp is a large sea plant that carries out photosynthesis. Sea urchins, mussels and fish feed on kelp. Starfish feed on mussels. Gulls feed on fish. Bald eagles feed on gulls. Sea otters feed on sea urchins and starfish.

(a) Use the statements to complete the food web. [4 marks]

(b) (i) Name the producer in this food web. [1 mark]
(ii) Name the organism that is both a secondary and tertiary consumer in this food web. [1 mark]

(c) Certain factors caused the population of sea otters to decrease in this area of the Pacific Ocean between 1987 and 1997. Use the graphs and the information provided to describe and explain what happened to the mass of kelp as a result of the decrease in the population of sea otters. [3 marks]

(d) In one other area of the Pacific Ocean, there were no sea otters. Scientists introduced some sea otters into this area and monitored their population from 1989 to 2017. The population of sea otters was 208 in 1989 and 2058 in 2017. Calculate the percentage (%) increase in the population of sea otters in this area between 1989 and 2017. Show your working out. Give your answer to two decimal places. [4 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a) [1] for kelp and sea otters in correct places in the food web
[1] for all other organisms (starfish, mussels, bald eagles, gulls, fish) in correct places
[1] for all 8 links drawn
[1] for correct arrows showing direction of energy flow
(b)(i) kelp
(b)(ii) sea otters
(c) Description: mass of kelp decreased
Explanation - any two from (comparisons required for each point):
more sea urchins / number of sea urchins increased / mass of sea urchins increased
fewer sea urchins eaten (by otters) / sea otters eat fewer sea urchins / sea urchins less predated on
more kelp eaten (by sea urchins) / more intake of kelp
(d) (2058 - 208) = 1850
1850 / 208 × 100
889.423077
889.42% — correct answer = [4] marks

topic: The Respiratory System, Breathing and Respiration
question:
Aerobic respiration is a process which takes place in body cells, to release energy. Describe the process of aerobic respiration in cells. Give two uses for the energy released in aerobic respiration in cells.

In this question you will be assessed on the quality of your written communication skills, including the use of specialist scientific terms. [6 marks]
mark_scheme:
Indicative content:
1. (uses) oxygen
2. (breaks down / uses) glucose
3. (produces / makes) carbon dioxide
4. (produces / makes) water
5. occurs in mitochondria / exothermic reaction
Any two uses of energy from:
6. movement / for muscles / for exercise / named exercise
7. growth / repair
8. reproduction
9. production of heat / maintaining body temperature / keep body temperature constant / keep body temperature at 37°C / warmth
10. active transport / active uptake
Must mention one use of energy to get into the top band.
Band A (5-6 marks): at least five points, excellent written communication
Band B (3-4 marks): at least three points, satisfactory written communication
Band C (1-2 marks): at least one point, limited written communication
Band D (0 marks): response not worthy of credit

question:
The excretory system carries out osmoregulation and produces urine.

(a) Name the organ that produces urine. [1 mark]

In an investigation, three students, A, B and C, each drank the same volume of water at the same time. Each student measured the volume of urine they produced at 10, 40, 70 and 100 minutes after drinking the water. The table shows some of the results for the volume of urine produced by students A, B and C at the different times (Student A: 10min=10, 40min=20, 70min=30, 100min=?; Student B: 10min=30, 40min=30, 70min=70, 100min=80; Student C: 10min=30, 40min=40, 70min=80, 100min=100 cm³). The bar chart shows some results for the total volume of urine produced by students A, B and C during the investigation (A=110 cm³, B=210 cm³).

(b) Use the table and the bar chart to calculate the volume of urine produced by student A at 100 minutes. Show your working out. [3 marks]

(c) (i) Use the table to calculate the total volume of urine produced by student C during the investigation. Show your working out. [2 marks]
(ii) Complete the bar chart by drawing the bar for the total volume of urine produced by student C during the investigation. Shade the bar you have drawn. [1 mark]

(d) (i) One student exercised vigorously just before drinking the water. Use the bar chart to identify this student and use data to explain your choice. [2 marks]
(ii) Give two reasons why exercising vigorously caused a change in the volume of urine produced by this student. [2 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a) kidney
(b) 110 (read from graph)
110 - 60
50 cm³ — correct answer = [3] marks
(c)(i) 30 + 40 + 80 + 100
250 cm³ — correct answer = [2] marks
(c)(ii) bar drawn to 250 (or e.c.f. from part (i))
(d)(i) Student A
Produced least / lowest amount of urine / produced less urine than both B and C / produced 100 less than B and 140 less than C (comparison required)
(d)(ii) (more water) lost in sweat / sweating (comparison required)
(more water) lost in breathing out / exhalation / expiration

question:
The diagrams show three experiments set up to investigate phototropism in plants.

(a) Use the instructions below to complete the boxes in the diagrams about the experiments.
Experiment 1: Add an arrow to show the direction of light shining on the shoots at the start of the experiment.
Experiment 2: Draw the three shoots at the start of the experiment. Add an arrow to show the direction of light shining on the shoots at the start of the experiment.
Experiment 3: Draw the three shoots as they would appear after four days.
(Experiment 1: normal shoots, light from above, shoots grow straight. Experiment 2: light from right side, shoots bend toward light. Experiment 3: foil covering shoot tips, light from right, shoots grow straight after four days.) [6 marks]

The diagram below shows the results of another experiment (Experiment 4): shoot with tip removed, light from above, shoots remain the same height with no bending after four days.

(b) Describe and explain the results shown after four days in experiment 4. [2 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a) Experiment 1: arrow coming straight down from above / from all directions
Experiment 2: arrow coming from the right; 3 shoots smaller/same size; shoots straight with no covering
Experiment 3: 3 shoots straight; covering on shoots / all shoots
[1] mark for each correct answer
(b) no growth / no cell elongation
no auxin / no hormone produced / no tip

question:
A student carried out an investigation into the effect of an inhibitor on an enzyme. The diagram represents the enzyme used. The diagrams below represent possible substrate molecules for this enzyme (A, B, C).

(a) (i) Give the letter of the diagram that represents the substrate molecule for this enzyme. [1 mark]
(ii) Give the letter of the diagram that represents the inhibitor molecule for this enzyme (X, Y, Z). [1 mark]

The student set up two test tubes. She added the same volumes of enzyme and substrate to both test tubes. No inhibitor was added to test tube 1 but inhibitor was added to test tube 2. She left both test tubes for 5 minutes. Test tube 1 (enzyme and substrate): large number of product molecules present. Test tube 2 (enzyme, substrate and inhibitor): very few product molecules present.

(b) Place a tick in the empty boxes to show all the statements that are true for this investigation.
The number of successful collisions of the substrate with the enzyme was: the same in test tubes 1 and 2 / greater in test tube 1 than in test tube 2 / less in test tube 1 than in test tube 2.
In test tube 2: some inhibitor joined to the active sites of the enzymes / no inhibitor joined to the active sites of the enzymes / some substrate joined to the active sites of the enzymes / no substrate joined to the active sites of the enzymes.
The rate of product formation in test tube 2 was: faster than test tube 1 / slower than test tube 1 / the same as test tube 1. [4 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) B
(a)(ii) Z
(b) Collisions: greater in test tube 1 than in test tube 2 [tick]
In test tube 2: some inhibitor joined to the active sites of the enzymes [tick]; some substrate joined to the active sites of the enzymes [tick]
Rate of product formation in test tube 2: slower than test tube 1 [tick]
[1] mark for each correct tick

question:
The diagram shows part of the nitrogen cycle with processes A, B, C, D and E labelled, involving nitrogen in the atmosphere, nitrate, plant protein, animal protein, and ammonia.

(a) Name processes A, B, C and D.
A _______________
B _______________
C _______________
D _______________ [4 marks]

(b) (i) Describe the type of soil conditions that reduce nitrate levels in the soil. [1 mark]
(ii) Name the type of nitrogen bacteria that are found in large numbers in this type of soil. [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a) A: nitrogen fixation
B: feeding / eating / consumption / ingestion
C: digestion / absorption / assimilation
D: nitrification
[1] mark each correct
(b)(i) anaerobic / lack of oxygen / waterlogged / flooded
(b)(ii) denitrifying

question:
The diagrams show possible examples of plant cells. Diagram A shows a root hair cell (with vacuole, nucleus, and extension). Diagrams B, C, D, E show variations — B has chloroplasts; C has no extension; D has chloroplasts and an extension with nucleus inside vacuole; E has no chloroplasts but has an extension with large nucleus.

(a) (i) Explain why diagram C cannot be a diagram of a root hair cell. [1 mark]
(ii) Explain why diagram D cannot be a diagram of a root hair cell. [1 mark]
(iii) Explain why diagram E cannot be a diagram of a root hair cell. [1 mark]

(b) Describe and explain the process of active uptake/transport in root hair cells. [4 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) has no extension
(a)(ii) has chloroplasts
(a)(iii) nucleus is inside vacuole / nucleus should be in the cytoplasm
(b) uptake of minerals (nitrates) / or named minerals / or ions
against a concentration gradient / or described / from low concentration (in soil) to high concentration (in the cell)
requires energy
needs respiration / oxygen

question:
Eutrophication occurred in a lake after the release of a pollutant containing nitrates. The boxes show the sequence of events that took place during eutrophication. Complete the sequence of events by writing on the lines in the boxes.

The pollutant, for example, _______________ entered the lake.
Aquatic plants _______________ due to the nitrates present in the pollutant.
Nitrate depletion and _______________ of aquatic plants occurred.
Aquatic plants died.
Aquatic plants were _______________ by bacteria.
These bacteria in the lake used up _______________.
Fish and aquatic animals died. [5 marks]
mark_scheme:
fertiliser / sewage / slurry / silage / human or animal waste / faeces / dirty yard water / milk
reproduced / multiplied / grew / increased / (algal) bloom
shading
decomposed
oxygen
[1] mark each in this order

question:
A student carried out an investigation into the effect of light intensity on leaf discs. He placed 10 leaf discs into a test tube with a solution that contained carbon dioxide and placed the test tube in dim light. When the leaf discs produced oxygen gas, they floated to the surface of the solution. The student measured the time taken for all the leaf discs to float to the surface of the solution. The student repeated the experiment at different light intensities. The graph shows the results (as light intensity increases from dim to bright, time taken for leaf discs to float decreases).

(a) (i) Describe the trend shown by these results. [1 mark]
(ii) Give an explanation for this trend. [1 mark]

(b) When all the leaf discs had floated to the surface of the solution, the student added hydrogencarbonate indicator to the test tube that had been in bright light. What colour was the solution in this test tube after hydrogencarbonate indicator was added? [1 mark]

This test tube (that had been in bright light) was then placed in the dark. Eventually, all the leaf discs sank to the bottom of the solution.

(c) (i) Choose the letters of the three statements that explain why all the leaf discs sank to the bottom of the solution. (A: the leaf discs became lighter; B: no oxygen was produced; C: oxygen was used up in respiration; D: oxygen was produced in respiration; E: the rate of respiration was the same as the rate of photosynthesis; F: the rate of photosynthesis was greater than the rate of respiration; G: there was no photosynthesis.) [3 marks]
(ii) What colour was the hydrogencarbonate indicator in this test tube after all the leaf discs sank to the bottom of the solution? [1 mark]
(iii) Choose the letter of the statement that explains the colour of the hydrogencarbonate indicator in this test tube when all the leaf discs sank to the bottom of the solution. (H: there was the same amount of carbon dioxide used as produced; I: there was no carbon dioxide used but carbon dioxide was produced; J: there was more carbon dioxide used than produced.) [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) as light intensity increased the time taken (for all the leaf discs to float to the surface of the solution) decreased; allow converse
(a)(ii) the more light for photosynthesis / more photosynthesis producing oxygen; allow converse
(b) purple
(c)(i) B, C, G (any order) [1] each
(c)(ii) yellow
(c)(iii) I

question:
The diagram shows part of the circulatory system in the body, with organs A and B, blood vessels C and D, and a pulmonary vein labelled.

(a) (i) Name organs A and B.
A _______________
B _______________ [2 marks]
(ii) Draw an arrow in the empty box on the diagram to show the direction of blood flow in the pulmonary vein. [1 mark]
(iii) Name blood vessels C and D.
C _______________
D _______________ [2 marks]

(b) Which type of blood vessel:
- has thin walls that are one cell thick?
- has valves? [2 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) A: lungs
B: kidney
(a)(ii) arrow pointing down beside pulmonary vein (toward the heart)
(a)(iii) C: hepatic portal vein
D: vena cava
(b) capillaries (thin walls one cell thick)
veins (have valves)

question:
The graph shows changes in the thickness of the uterus lining during a 28-day menstrual cycle.

(a) (i) Between which days in the menstrual cycle, shown above, does menstruation occur?
Between days ___ and ___ [1 mark]
(ii) Give the day when ovulation usually occurs in a 28-day menstrual cycle.
Day ___ [1 mark]
(iii) Describe what happens to the uterus lining after day 28 if fertilisation does not occur. [1 mark]

(b) Contraception can be used to prevent pregnancy.
(i) Suggest one ethical reason why people may choose not to use contraception. [1 mark]
(ii) Name one STI caused by bacteria. [1 mark]
(iii) Name one STI caused by viruses. [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) 1 and 5
(a)(ii) day 14
(a)(iii) (uterus lining) breaks down / thickness decreases / lining sheds
(b)(i) appropriate religious / moral reason (e.g. religious belief that contraception is wrong, belief that life begins at conception)
(b)(ii) chlamydia / gonorrhoea / syphilis
(b)(iii) AIDS / HIV / HPV / herpes

topic: Health, Disease, Defence Mechanisms and Treatments
question:
The body defends itself against disease-causing microorganisms, including bacteria, in several ways.

(a) Complete the sentences about some of the ways the body defends itself against bacteria.
The skin provides a _______________ against bacteria.
Mucous membranes in the nose and throat _______________ bacteria.
Blood _______________ stops the loss of blood and prevents the entry of bacteria. [3 marks]

(b) Another type of defence against bacteria involves lymphocytes. The diagram shows a bacterium and the chemicals produced by lymphocytes after infection by this bacterium.
(i) Name the chemicals produced by lymphocytes. [1 mark]

If a person is infected again with the same type of bacteria, a secondary response is produced. Therefore, this person does not develop any symptoms.
(ii) Explain why. [3 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a) (physical) barrier
trap / filter / catch
clotting
(b)(i) antibody / antibodies
(b)(ii) Any three from:
they are immune
memory cells / memory lymphocytes
(memory cells/lymphocytes) remain in the blood for a long time / produced for a long time
high / large / greater number of antibodies
antibodies are produced faster / quickly / act quickly
antibodies produced for long(er) period of time

topic: Health, Disease, Defence Mechanisms and Treatments
question:
Chemicals, such as carbon monoxide, in tobacco smoke have harmful effects on the body. Name the other two main chemicals in tobacco smoke and describe the harmful effects of each on the body.

In this question you will be assessed on the quality of your written communication skills, including the use of specialist scientific terms.

Name and harmful effects of chemical 1:
_______________

Name and harmful effects of chemical 2:
_______________ [6 marks]
mark_scheme:
Indicative content:
1. tar
2. causes bronchitis / narrowing of bronchi and bronchioles
3. causes emphysema / damage to alveoli / reducing the surface area of alveoli / lungs (for gas exchange)
4. causes lung cancer / abnormal cell division in lungs
5. nicotine
6. addictive
7. affects heart rate / pulse rate
[1] mark for each effect linked with the correct substance
Band A (5-6 marks): at least five points, excellent written communication
Band B (3-4 marks): at least three points, satisfactory written communication
Band C (1-2 marks): at least one point, limited written communication
Band D (0 marks): not worthy of credit

question:
Transpiration is the evaporation of water from cells in a leaf followed by diffusion into the atmosphere. The diagram shows part of a leaf.
Water evaporates from the cells, labelled A, in a leaf.

(a) (i) Name the cells labelled A. [1 mark]
(ii) Name the two parts of the leaf that water diffuses through after it evaporates from the cells labelled A. [2 marks]

(b) Several environmental factors affect the rate of transpiration. One of these factors is light intensity. The graph shows how light intensity affects the rate of transpiration (positive linear relationship).
(i) Describe the trend shown by the graph. [1 mark]
(ii) On the axes below, draw a line to show how increasing temperature affects the rate of transpiration. [1 mark]
(iii) Name one other environmental factor that affects the rate of transpiration. [1 mark]

The diagram shows a potometer which can be used to investigate water uptake by a plant shoot.
(c) Describe and explain what will happen to the air bubble as transpiration occurs. [3 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) spongy mesophyll
(a)(ii) air spaces; stomata (any order)
(b)(i) as light intensity increases, rate of transpiration increases
(b)(ii) line showing positive linear relationship (rate of transpiration increases with temperature)
(b)(iii) wind (speed) / humidity
(c) (bubble) moves towards plant / to the left
Any two from:
water moves out of leaf / shoot / plant / stomata
by diffusion / evaporation
water is replaced / to replace water lost

question:
Some couples may use in vitro fertilisation (IVF) treatment to help the woman become pregnant.

(a) (i) Suggest why a woman with blocked oviducts may use IVF to help her become pregnant. [1 mark]
(ii) Suggest one other reason why a woman may use IVF to help her become pregnant. [1 mark]

(b) Hormones are given to a woman at the start of the IVF process. Explain why. [1 mark]

(c) The diagram shows some of the stages of IVF (Stage 1: fertilisation producing zygote; Stage 2: cell divides into two cells; Stage 3: ball of cells/embryo; Stage 4: Process X in uterus lining; Stage 5: ball of cells produces a variety of tissues and organs).
(i) Name the organs that produce male and female gametes.
Male gametes _______________
Female gametes _______________ [2 marks]
(ii) Name the cell produced by fertilisation at stage 1. [1 mark]
(iii) Name the ball of identical cells produced at stage 3. [1 mark]
(iv) Name the process X that occurs at stage 4. [1 mark]
(v) Name the process that occurs at stage 5. [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) sperm cannot reach the egg / fertilisation cannot occur
(a)(ii) lack of eggs / ovulation / she is infertile / hostile vagina
(b) to produce several eggs / more ovulation
(c)(i) testes (male); ovaries (female) — in this order
(c)(ii) zygote
(c)(iii) embryo
(c)(iv) implantation
(c)(v) differentiation

topic: Health, Disease, Defence Mechanisms and Treatments
question:
An investigation was carried out into antibiotic resistance in one type of bacteria. Scientists grew bacteria on agar in a Petri dish. The diagram shows the stages and some of the results of the investigation. An antibiotic was added after stage B. Stage C has not been completed on the diagram. (Stage A: a few original bacteria; Stage B after 24 hours: original bacteria increased, plus antibiotic-resistant bacteria appear; Stage C — 24 hours after antibiotic added — not completed; Stage D 48 hours after antibiotic added: many antibiotic-resistant bacteria only).

(a) (i) Use the diagram to describe two changes between stages A and B. [2 marks]
(ii) Complete stage C on the diagram by drawing what you would expect to see 24 hours after the antibiotic was added. [3 marks]

In an investigation, patients in hospitals in the United Kingdom (UK) between 2007 and 2016 had blood samples taken to check for the presence of MRSA bacteria. The graph shows the number of patients with MRSA when they were admitted to hospital (2007/8 to 2015/16), and the number of patients who developed MRSA in hospital. The figures given are per 1000 patients.

(b) Complete the table using only the information in the graph. Place a tick in one empty box for each statement. (Statements: The trends for both groups of patients were the same; 45 patients per 1000 were admitted with MRSA in 2007/8; More patients were admitted with MRSA in 2003/4 than in 2007/8; Each year, more patients developed MRSA in hospital than were admitted with MRSA.) [4 marks]

(c) (i) Suggest two measures that staff in hospitals could have used to reduce the spread of MRSA bacteria. [2 marks]
(ii) Suggest one measure that staff in hospitals could have used to kill MRSA bacteria. [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) doubled / replicated / multiplied / reproduced / more bacteria
presence of antibiotic resistant bacteria
(a)(ii) black circles drawn; four black circles drawn; no clear circles drawn
(b) The trends for both groups were the same: True
45 patients per 1000 admitted with MRSA in 2007/8: False
More patients admitted with MRSA in 2003/4 than in 2007/8: Cannot tell
Each year, more patients developed MRSA in hospital than were admitted with MRSA: False
[1] mark for each correct tick as long as not multiple ticks in the same row
(c)(i) Any two from: use gloves / gowns / masks / PPE; wash hands (between patients) / use hand sanitiser; patient isolation; clean up spills / use antiseptics / use disinfectants
(c)(ii) use of hand sanitisers / use of antiseptics / use of disinfectants

topic: Genome, Chromosomes, Genes, DNA and Genetics
question:
The photograph shows a peacock (male) and a peahen (female). Peacocks have brightly coloured fronts and tail feathers. In some peacocks and peahens, the main body colour is brown. In other peacocks and peahens, the main body colour is white. The allele for brown body colour (A) is dominant to the allele for white body colour (a). Main body colour in peacocks and peahens is not sex-linked.

(a) Use the letters A and a to complete the diagram for the inheritance of the main body colour in peacocks and peahens over two generations. Write the correct letter or letters in the empty circles. Two circles have been completed for you (white parent = aa, white gamete = a). The cross is between a brown parent and white parent (aa), producing brown F1 offspring, which when crossed produce 3 brown : 1 white in F2. [4 marks]

(b) A breeder had a peacock with a brown main body colour. Describe how he could carry out a test (back) cross to determine the genotype of this peacock. [3 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a) Parental genotype brown = AA; gametes from brown = A; gametes from white = a
F1 genotypes = Aa and Aa (both brown)
F2 gametes: A, a, A, a
F2 genotypes: AA, Aa, Aa, aa (brown, brown, brown, white)
circles correct in first two rows = [1] mark; all circles in each of the three rows that follow correct = [1] mark per row
(b) cross his brown peacock with a white peahen
if any white offspring then his peacock was Aa / heterozygous
if all the offspring were brown then his peacock was AA / homozygous dominant

topic: Genome, Chromosomes, Genes, DNA and Genetics
question:
The diagram shows the chromosomes in the nucleus of a cell before cell division (2 pairs of chromosomes: one large curved pair and one small straight pair).

The diagrams below show different chromosome combinations (numbered 1-9, showing various combinations of these chromosomes in nuclei).

(a) (i) Choose the numbers of all the diagrams that show the nuclei that could be produced if this cell divided by meiosis. Write the correct numbers on the line below. [3 marks]
(ii) Name the process by which the chromosomes in the gametes are randomly distributed. [1 mark]

(b) Give two reasons why mitosis is important in multicellular organisms. [2 marks]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) 2, 4, 5 and 8
If not, [1] mark for any 4 numbers; if the four numbers are 2468 / 2456 / 4568 / 2568 = [2] marks; [1] mark only if there are five numbers 24568
(a)(ii) independent assortment
(b) Any two from:
growth
renew / replace (worn out) cells
repair (damaged tissue)

topic: Genome, Chromosomes, Genes, DNA and Genetics
question:
Questions about DNA structure and genetic engineering (based on mark scheme evidence for Q10).

(a) (i) A diagram shows different representations of the DNA double helix. Give the letter of the diagram that correctly represents the DNA double helix. [1 mark]
(ii) Name the two components of the backbone of a DNA strand (other than the bases). [2 marks]

(b) A section of DNA contains 35% adenine (A) bases.
(i) Calculate the percentage of cytosine (C) bases in this section of DNA. Show your working out. [3 marks]
(ii) A strand of DNA contains 6 base pairs. How many bases are on one strand of this DNA? [1 mark]

(c) The diagram shows a DNA sequence being cut by a restriction enzyme.
(i) Draw lines on the diagram to show where the restriction enzyme cuts the DNA sequence to produce staggered cuts. [3 marks]
(ii) How many fragments of DNA are produced when the restriction enzyme cuts the DNA at all the recognition sites shown? [1 mark]
mark_scheme:
(a)(i) C
(a)(ii) sugar / deoxyribose; phosphate (either order)
(b)(i) 35 + 35 = 70
100 - 70 = 30
30 / 2 = 15
15% — correct answer = [3] marks
(b)(ii) 6
(c)(i) [1] mark for each staggered cut (3 staggered cuts shown in the recognition sequences GCATG/CGTA along the DNA)
(c)(ii) 4
